results.possibleIdentity: Peter Ballantine is an American artist, master woodworker, and fabricator who has been active in the New York art world since the late 1960s. He is best known for his long-term collaboration with the Minimalist artist Donald Judd, for whom he served as the primary fabricator of plywood works from 1971 until Judd's death in 1994. Ballantine grew up in the suburbs of Chicago and studied art in Colorado before moving to New York in 1968 to participate as a fellow in the Whitney Museum of American Art's Independent Study Program (ISP). He began working for Judd in April 1969, initially performing carpentry and renovation on Judd's building at 101 Spring Street. In the early 1970s, Ballantine was associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) when he was commissioned to design and build the mahogany boxes used to house 'The New York Collection for Stockholm,' a portfolio of 30 prints by prominent New York artists donated to the Moderna Museet. Following Judd's death, Ballantine served as the art supervisor for the Judd Estate and the Judd Foundation (1994–2004) and has since worked as a freelance curator, restorer, and lecturer. He is also the founding director of the Judd-Hume Prize, a research fellowship at the University of Edinburgh focusing on the intersection of art and philosophy.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (sometimes phonetically confused as Agnus Dewes) is a prominent conceptual, environmental, and ecological artist who rose to international attention in the late 1960s and 1970s. She is widely recognized as a pioneer of several art movements, including Land Art and Computer Art. Her work is characterized by a deep integration of science, mathematics, philosophy, linguistics, and technology. Notably, she was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, having submitted proposals for collaborations in 1969 and 1970, including those for the Pepsi Pavilion. Her artistic practice often involves collaborating with engineers and scientists; for example, in 1970 she participated in the influential 'Software' exhibition at the Jewish Museum in New York, where she worked with the computer scientist group R.E.S.I.S.T.O.R.S. She is perhaps most famous for her 1982 work 'Wheatfield – A Confrontation,' where she planted two acres of wheat on a landfill in lower Manhattan near the World Trade Center. Her oeuvre includes series such as 'Philosophical Drawings' (begun in 1969), 'Map Projections' (begun in 1973), and the 'Pyramid Series' (begun in 1970), which utilize isometric projections and mathematical logic to visualize complex human knowledge systems.

results.possibleIdentity: Judy Padow (also known as Judith Padow) is an American choreographer and dancer who was a prominent figure in the New York postmodern dance scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. She was closely associated with the Judson Dance Theater and its circle of experimental artists. Padow was a founding member of the Natural History of the American Dancer (an improvisational group formed in 1970) and performed extensively with the Lucinda Childs Dance Company, appearing in landmark works such as "Calico Mingling" (1973) and "Reclining Rondo" (1975). In the early 1970s, she founded the Judy Padow Dance Company, for which she choreographed numerous works including "Cameo" (1977), "Indian Summer" (1977), and "Trio in Unison" (1977), the latter featuring music by Philip Glass. Her work often involved collaborations with leading avant-garde figures like Glass, Yvonne Rainer, and Mary Overlie. She is also linked to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); notably, she participated in the "Tape-Video Live" performance in 1972 which was taped at E.A.T.’s Automation House studio. Additionally, she was involved in the administration of the New York dance community, serving as a co-director of the Danspace Project at St. Mark's Church. Later in her career, she transitioned to labor law, practicing as an attorney since the late 1980s while maintaining her connection to the arts through creative writing.

results.possibleIdentity: Ronald Mallory (1932–2021) was an American architect and artist who became a prominent figure in the kinetic art movement of the 1960s and 1970s. Born in Philadelphia, he studied architecture at the University of Florida and worked in Rio de Janeiro for modernist architect Oscar Niemeyer and landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx. Transitioning to the visual arts, Mallory moved to Paris in the 1950s before returning to New York City, where he shared a studio with kinetic artist Pol Bury. He is best known for his 'mercury pictures'—kinetic sculptures created by sealing liquid mercury between layers of acrylic or Plexiglas, often powered by slow-moving motors to create ever-changing fluid compositions. Mallory was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, and his name appears in the E.A.T. sub-series of the Robert Rauschenberg papers. His work is held in major permanent collections, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (SFMOMA). He was also known in social circles as a 'jet setter' and was married to actress and model Carole Mallory from 1968 to 1971.

results.possibleIdentity: Stafford Beer (1926–2002), often referred to as Dr. Stafford Beer or Professor Stafford Beer, was a British cyberneticist and management theorist who was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. While primarily recognized as the founder of management cybernetics, Beer was a polymath who integrated art, poetry, and philosophy into his scientific and technical work. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle; his theories on cybernetics and feedback systems provided a theoretical framework for the 'Art and Technology' movement of the era. His work was specifically cited in the context of the E.A.T. exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' (Brooklyn Museum, 1968–1969). Additionally, Beer participated in the landmark exhibition 'Cybernetic Serendipity' (ICA London, 1968) alongside fellow cyberneticist Gordon Pask. As an artist, he produced poetry—including a poem titled 'SAM' (after his Stochastic Analogue Machine)—and paintings, such as his 'Requiem' series (I–IX) created after he moved to Wales in the mid-1970s. His most famous technical project, Project Cybersyn in Chile (1971–1973), involved the creation of a 'socialist internet' and featured a futuristic Operations Room ('Opsroom') designed with high aesthetic and ergonomic standards.
